model.label: MiMo-V2-Flash
model.description: MiMo • Flash-tier model configured through a custom endpoint for fast inference and lightweight agent tasks.

pick.rationale: Michigan holds a razor-thin edge in efficiency metrics, ranking 2nd nationally in both KenPom and Torvik with elite defense and a top-10 offense, while Arizona ranks 3rd with a slightly less potent attack. The Wolverines' defensive prowess, anchored by a top-2 adjusted defense, should disrupt Arizona's high-powered offense, and Michigan's recent form—including a 31-2 record and wins over top competition—provides a reliable path to victory. While Arizona has a credible upset path through their offensive firepower and depth, Michigan's superior matchup dynamics and balanced attack make them the more reliable pick in this Final Four showdown.

reasoningStep.title: Efficiency and Matchup Analysis
reasoningStep.summary: Both teams are elite, but Michigan's defensive edge and slightly higher overall ranking give them a narrow advantage in a tight matchup.
reasoningStep.evidence: Michigan ranks 2nd in KenPom and Torvik with adjD of 88.4 (KenPom) and 90.5 (Torvik), while Arizona ranks 3rd with adjD of 89.7 (KenPom) and 91.1 (Torvik). Michigan's offense is also highly efficient, ranking 8th in KenPom adjO. No major injury concerns were found for either team, and prior picks show Michigan advancing through a tough path.

toolCalls.summary: Returned combined Torvik/KenPom ratings for 2 teams.

result.text: Key March Madness injuries to know while filling out your men’s NCAA Tournament bracket - The Athletic Premier League Champions League 2026 Men's World Cup F1 Tennis FA Cup Cricket La Liga Europa League Women's Football Olympics Global Sports Cycling Sailing Sports Business Boxing Golf Serie A Bundesliga International Football NFL NBA MLB World Baseball Classic NHL MLS Transfer News FPL League Cup Championship League One League Two Women's Euros NWSL College Sports NCAAF NCAAM Peak MMA Motorsports NASCAR Culture Memorabilia & Collectibles Gaming Sports Betting The PL Is Being Left Behind Welcome to Chelsea, Liam Rosenior PL Player of the Year Nominees Log In Subscribe Now Tickets by Viagogo Connections: Sports Edition REGION U.S. International Search Top News Podcasts NCAAM Bracket Scores & Schedule Teams Alabama Arizona Arkansas Duke Florida Gonzaga Houston Illinois Iowa State Kansas Louisville Miami (FL) Michigan State Michigan Nebraska North Carolina Purdue Saint Mary's St. John's Tennessee Texas Tech UConn Vanderbilt Virginia Wisconsin Standings Beat Our Expert's Bracket Tournament Projections Printable Bracket Predictions Game Men's March Madness LIVE First Four Updates Tournament Forecast Challenge Our Experts The Perfect Bracket Bracket Busters NBA Prospects to Watch Key Injuries To Know Key March Madness injuries to know while filling out your men’s NCAA Tournament bracket Patrick Ngongba II and Duke are the overall No. 1 seed, but a couple of injuries could make it more vulnerable. Grant Halverson / Getty Images By Jim Root March 17, 2026 Updated 3:19 pm EDT Share full article 2 The Athletic has live coverage of the First Four from 2026 Men’s March Madness. Roster attrition is a factor during the postseason in any sport. Optimistically, college basketball’s shorter regular season should mean the title picture is less altered by injuries than, say, the NBA. That is not necessarily the case this season, though. The injury bug has recently bitten two No. 1 seeds (Duke and Michigan), and a third (Arizona) played through a flurry of nagging injuries late in the season. Multiple All-Americans on dark-horse title contenders are out for the season. Advertisement Below is a repository of all the biggest injuries impacting the men’s NCAA Tournament. We have split them into two categories: Injuries worth monitoring (the players could or will return at some point during the tournament) and out for the season. Injuries to monitor Caleb Foster and Patrick Ngongba II, Duke Both Duke starters missed the ACC tournament. The Blue Devils managed to win that event regardless, but they might need some reinforcements to handle the competition they will see if they reach the Elite Eight and beyond. Ngongba, out with a foot injury, is arguably the more valuable piece due to his defensive presence, elite finishing and sneaky-strong passing. Fortunately for the Blue Devils, he is also much closer to returning than Foster, though

Boston College Eagles 2d Jeff Borzello Jay Bilas predicts every game of the 2026 NCAA tournament play 'College GameDay' crew makes its Final Four picks (2:42) Jay Williams, Jay Bilas, Seth Greenberg and Rece Davis reveal their picks for the 2026 men's NCAA tournament. (2:42) Jay Bilas Mar 16, 2026, 09:00 AM ET Close College basketball analyst for ESPN and ESPN Insider Played and coached at Duke Practicing attorney Multiple Authors Email Print Open Extended Reactions You already know the drill. After my fellow analysts and I have the privilege of calling the SEC championship game in Nashville, Tennessee, we move swiftly to process the men's NCAA tournament field as it is revealed. Our bosses at ESPN ask us each to fill out a bracket -- while we are on the air -- so we can reveal our picks before the end of the show. They give us less than five minutes. Hardly ideal, but perhaps our first instincts are better than considered thought. I decided that I would give you more than five minutes so you can dazzle your friends and win your office pool -- and perhaps our Tournament Challenge. Last season, my bracket was chalky : I had all four No. 1 seeds reaching the Final Four, Houston and Florida advancing to the championship game, and the Gators cutting down the nets. The backlash was swift and unrelenting. I was criticized for not picking an underdog to reach San Antonio. B

modelTrace.content:
You are an analyst predicting a single March Madness game inside a bracket run.
You have live tools. Use them before making a pick when they can reduce uncertainty.
You are not filling the whole bracket in one response. Predict only the current game.
Use prior picks as already committed bracket state.
You may use at most 10 tool rounds before you must finalize your answer.
Before making a pick, investigate relevant context such as injuries or availability concerns, recent team news, prior head-to-head results when useful, and multiple raw statistics from the available ratings and matchup data.
Take a holistic approach. Balance team quality, matchup specifics, health, form, schedule context, and upset risk instead of relying on one metric.
Do not force an upset, but explicitly consider whether the underdog has a credible path to win and let that affect confidence and rationale.
Return strict JSON only.
Final JSON keys: pick, reasoningStep.
pick must contain gameId, winnerId, confidence, rationale.
The rationale must be a short paragraph of 2 to 4 sentences explaining why the winner was chosen.
reasoningStep must contain id, title, summary, evidence.
Bracket config id: demo-2026-bracket
Tournament year: 2026
Current game id: f4-2
Current game label: National semifinal 2
Round: Final Four
Resolved teams: Arizona (arizona) vs Michigan (michigan)
Prior committed picks: 65

Boston College Eagles 2d Jeff Borzello Jay Bilas predicts every game of the 2026 NCAA tournament play 'College GameDay' crew makes its Final Four picks (2:42) Jay Williams, Jay Bilas, Seth Greenberg and Rece Davis reveal their picks for the 2026 men's NCAA tournament. (2:42) Jay Bilas Mar 16, 2026, 09:00 AM ET Close College basketball analyst for ESPN and ESPN Insider Played and coached at Duke Practicing attorney Multiple Authors Email Print Open Extended Reactions You already know the drill. After my fellow analysts and I have the privilege of calling the SEC championship game in Nashville, Tennessee, we move swiftly to process the men's NCAA tournament field as it is revealed. Our bosses at ESPN ask us each to fill out a bracket -- while we are on the air -- so we can reveal our picks before the end of the show. They give us less than five minutes. Hardly ideal, but perhaps our first instincts are better than considered thought. I decided that I would give you more than five minutes so you can dazzle your friends and win your office pool -- and perhaps our Tournament Challenge. Last season, my bracket was chalky : I had all four No. 1 seeds reaching the Final Four, Houston and Florida advancing to the championship game, and the Gators cutting down the nets. The backlash was swift and unrelenting. I was criticized for not picking an underdog to reach San Antonio. B
